skill.name: azure-ai-document-intelligence-dotnet
skill.description: Guides .NET developers using Azure AI Document Intelligence to analyze documents, extract fields, read layouts, build custom models, and manage classifiers.
skill.summary: Use Azure AI Document Intelligence from .NET for OCR, structured extraction, prebuilt models, and custom model workflows.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static command-execution and weak-cryptography findings are false positives caused by Markdown code fences, inline code, and model names. The real risks are legitimate but sensitive: examples use Azure network services, environment variables for API keys, and SAS URLs for training data. No prompt injection attempt or confirmed malicious behavior was found.

medium_findings.title: Sensitive Document Data Sent to Azure Services
medium_findings.description: The skill teaches users to submit document URLs to Azure AI Document Intelligence for analysis. This is expected behavior for the SDK, but users may process invoices, receipts, IDs, or health insurance cards that contain sensitive data. Organizations should confirm data handling, region, and retention requirements before use.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code examples directly call Azure analysis APIs with document URIs. This is legitimate SDK usage, but the document categories shown include sensitive business and personal information.

medium_findings.title: API Key and SAS URL Handling Requires Care
medium_findings.description: The examples read an API key from an environment variable and use SAS URLs for blob training data. This is a standard Azure pattern, but leaked keys or overly broad SAS tokens could expose document data or allow unauthorized service usage.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The environment variable and SAS URL examples are explicit in the skill text. They are not malicious, but misuse can expose credentials or protected training documents.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified as Code Execution
low_findings.description: Most static external command findings are caused by Markdown code fences and inline C# identifiers. The file is instructional Markdown and does not contain a Ruby program or executable shell logic. The only actual shell command examples install official NuGet packages.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged text is visible inside Markdown examples, not executable skill code. No file other than SKILL.md exists in the analyzed results.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Documentation and Placeholder Examples
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs point to Azure documentation, NuGet, Document Intelligence Studio, or placeholder example documents. No evidence found that the skill sends secrets to an unrelated or hidden endpoint.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are either placeholders or expected official resource links. The only non-documentation host shown for document input is example.com.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Matches Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The static weak-cryptography alerts map to prose, table rows, or SDK type names. No evidence found of MD5, SHA-1, DES, RC4, or another weak cryptographic algorithm being used or recommended.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Review of the cited lines shows model names, descriptions, and C# field access. They do not contain cryptographic code or algorithm recommendations.

low_findings.title: Dangerous Combination Heuristic Dismissed
low_findings.description: The scanner combined command, network, and credential signals into a critical heuristic. Context shows documentation examples for package installation, Azure endpoint use, and credential loading. No evidence found of obfuscation, exfiltration, prompt injection, or hidden execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: All three signal classes are explainable by SDK documentation. The analyzed file is plain Markdown with no separate scripts or encoded payloads.

dangerous_patterns.title: Environment Variable API Key Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill shows API key authentication from an environment variable. This is a common Azure pattern, but key rotation and secret storage controls are required.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines clearly read an API key from the environment and pass it to AzureKeyCredential. The pattern is legitimate but security-sensitive.

dangerous_patterns.title: Blob SAS URL Training Source Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill uses blob SAS URLs for custom model and classifier training data. SAS tokens should be scoped, short-lived, and excluded from prompts and logs.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines create blob content sources from SAS-style URLs. This is expected for Azure training workflows, but it can expose storage if mishandled.

content.value_statement: Document extraction projects need correct SDK patterns for OCR, layout, invoices, receipts, and custom models. This skill gives .NET developers focused Azure AI Document Intelligence examples and workflow guidance.
actual_capabilities: Shows how to install Azure.AI.DocumentIntelligence and Azure.Identity packages.
Explains Entra ID and API key authentication patterns for .NET clients.
Provides examples for invoices, receipts, layout extraction, and document classification.
Covers custom document model and classifier build workflows using blob sources.
Lists important SDK types, model identifiers, build modes, and reference links.
limitations: It is a reference guide and does not run analysis by itself.
Examples require an Azure Document Intelligence resource and valid credentials.
It does not cover every SDK option, region rule, or pricing detail.
Users must adapt examples for privacy, compliance, and production error handling.

use_cases.title: Invoice Extraction Service
use_cases.description: Build a .NET service that extracts vendors, totals, and line items from invoices using the prebuilt invoice model.

use_cases.title: Document Layout Review
use_cases.description: Extract page text, tables, and structure from PDFs before sending data to downstream systems.

use_cases.title: Custom Model Training
use_cases.description: Create custom document models or classifiers from labeled files stored in Azure Blob Storage.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Client
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me create a minimal .NET setup for Azure AI Document Intelligence using Entra ID authentication.

prompt_templates.title: Extract Invoice Fields
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me how to use the prebuilt invoice model in .NET and explain how to read vendor, total, and line item fields.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Custom Training
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me design a custom model training workflow using Azure Blob Storage and Document Intelligence build modes.

prompt_templates.title: Review Production Risks
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my planned Document Intelligence .NET integration for credential handling, SAS token scope, privacy, retries, and error handling.

output_examples.input: User asks how to extract fields from an invoice PDF.
output_examples.output: A step-by-step .NET workflow using the prebuilt invoice model, including the key fields to inspect and confidence checks to perform.

output_examples.input: User asks which model to use for mixed document types.
output_examples.output: A recommendation to build or use a classifier first, then route each document to the correct analysis model.

output_examples.input: User asks how to train a custom model from stored files.
output_examples.output: A concise plan for preparing blob storage, selecting template or neural build mode, starting a build operation, and reviewing model details.

best_practices: Prefer Entra ID authentication for production applications when the service configuration supports it.
Use short-lived, least-privilege SAS tokens and never include real tokens in prompts or logs.
Check field confidence and handle long-running operations, retries, and service errors explicitly.
anti_patterns: Do not hardcode API keys, endpoints, or SAS tokens in source files.
Do not send regulated documents to Azure before confirming compliance and data residency requirements.
Do not assume every extracted field is correct without confidence checks and validation rules.

faq.question: What does this skill help me build?
faq.answer: It helps you build .NET applications that use Azure AI Document Intelligence for OCR and structured document extraction.

faq.question: Does the skill process documents directly?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples. Your application sends documents to your configured Azure resource.

faq.question: Which authentication method should I use?
faq.answer: Use Microsoft Entra ID for production when possible. API keys are supported but require strong secret management.

faq.question: Can it help with invoices and receipts?
faq.answer: Yes. The guide includes prebuilt invoice and receipt model workflows for common field extraction tasks.

faq.question: Can it help with custom document formats?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers custom model and classifier build workflows using Azure Blob Storage sources.

faq.question: What security precautions matter most?
faq.answer: Protect API keys, scope SAS URLs tightly, avoid logging sensitive document data, and review Azure compliance settings.
